You are working on the Kotlin/kotlinx.coroutines repository.
The codebase is at version 1.6 (commit: 1ed19c87).

## Issue to Resolve

Consider deprecating or changing the behaviour of CoroutineContext.isActive
According to the [doc](https://kotlin.github.io/kotlinx.coroutines/kotlinx-coroutines-core/kotlinx.coroutines/is-active.html), `isActive` has the following property:

>The coroutineContext.isActive expression is a shortcut for coroutineContext[Job]?.isActive == true. See [Job.isActive](https://kotlin.github.io/kotlinx.coroutines/kotlinx-coroutines-core/kotlinx.coroutines/-job/is-active.html).

It means that, if the `Job` is not present, `isActive` always returns `false`.

We have multiple reports that such behaviour can be error-prone when used with non-`kotlinx.coroutines` entry points, such as Ktor and `suspend fun main`, because it is inconsistent with the overall contract:

>(Job) has not been completed and was not cancelled yet


`CoroutineContext.isActive` predates both `CoroutineScope` (which should always have a `Job` in it, if it's not `GlobalScope`)  and `job` extension, so it may be the case that it can be safely deprecated.

Basically, we have three options:

* Do nothing, left things as is. It doesn't solve the original issue, but also doesn't introduce any potentially breaking changes
* Deprecate `CoroutineContext.isActive`. Such change has multiple potential downsides
    * Its only possible replacement is `this.job.isActive`, but this replacement is not equivalent to the original method -- `.job` throws an exception for contexts without a `Job`. An absence of replacement can be too disturbing as [a lot of code](https://grep.app/search?q=context.isActive&filter[lang][0]=Kotlin) rely on a perfectly fine `ctxWithJob.isActive`
    * Code that relies on `.job.isActive` no longer can be called from such entry points safely
* Change the default behaviour -- return `true`. It also "fixes" such patterns as `GlobalScope.isActive` but basically is a breaking change

## Additional Context from Issue Discussion

> It also "fixes" such patterns as `GlobalScope.isActive`

`GlobalScope.isActive` is not the same property, and already defaults to `true` when there is no job, so this one is not broken:
https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx.coroutines/blob/master/kotlinx-coroutines-core/common/src/CoroutineScope.kt#L137

This is why I believe it's often more correct to use `CoroutineScope.isActive` rather than `CoroutineContext.isActive`.

> Its only possible replacement is `this.job.isActive`

Why, though? Isn't `this[Job]?.isActive == true` another perfectly valid replacement that doesn't change behaviour? Or is this what you mean by "absence of replacement" - just inlining the property?

I would also be reluctant in suggesting the `.job.isActive` replacement. Very often the authors of those pieces of code just didn't think of the jobless case, and here we would push them towards making their code fail even harder in this case (possibly unknowingly).

> An absence of replacement can be too disturbing as [a lot of code](https://grep.app/search?q=context.isActive&filter%5Blang%5D%5B0%5D=Kotlin) rely on a perfectly fine `ctxWithJob.isActive`

Without better-looking replacement than the body of the current property getter, users could "fix" their code by wrapping the body of their function with `coroutineScope { ... }` and accessing the `CoroutineScope.isActive` property from that scope (which would now have a job). Or just use `yield()` if they want to cancel a non-suspending loop. Or just remove that condition altogether because they didn't need it in the first place (calling other suspend functions with prompt cancellation guarantees already).

Whether we should deprecate `CoroutineContext.isActive` is rather a conceptual matter of whether we want to give meaning to this property when there is no job.

---
TL;DR: I like both the deprecation option and the default change option, but if we deprecate I find the replacement `.job.isActive` harmful. What I like about the change of default is that even though it's technically breaking, it will probably fix more things than break them, and without any change in the ecosystem but version bumps.

## Relevant Files

The following files are likely relevant to solving this issue:
- `kotlinx-coroutines-core/common/src/Job.kt`

Please analyze this issue and implement a fix. Make the necessary code changes to resolve the problem described above.

Focus on:

Let me understand the issue:

1. `CoroutineContext.isActive` currently returns `false` when there's no `Job` in the context (because it's `coroutineContext[Job]?.isActive == true`)
2. This is error-prone when used with non-kotlinx.coroutines entry points like Ktor and `suspend fun main` where there might not be a Job
3. The issue is that `false` doesn't align with the contract: "has not been completed and was not cancelled yet" - if there's no job, it hasn't been completed or cancelled

The options discussed:
1. Do nothing
2. Deprecate `CoroutineContext.isActive` - but the replacement `.job.isActive` throws if no job, which is worse
3. Change the default behavior to return `true` when there's no job

From the discussion, it seems like option 3 (changing the default to return `true`) is preferred because:
- `CoroutineScope.isActive` already defaults to `true` when there's no job
- It will "fix more things than break them"
- It aligns better with the conceptual meaning (no job = not completed/cancelled = active)

 15â†’// --------------- core job interfaces ---------------
 16â†’
 17â†’/**
 18â†’ * A background job. Conceptually, a job is a cancellable thing with a life-cycle that
 19â†’ * culminates in its completion.
 20â†’ *
 21â†’ * Jobs can be arranged into parent-child hierarchies where cancellation
 22â†’ * of a parent leads to immediate cancellation of all its [children] recursively.
 23â†’ * Failure of a child with an exception other than [CancellationException] immediately cancels its parent and,
 24â†’ * consequently, all its other children. This behavior can be customized using [SupervisorJob].
 25â†’ *
 26â†’ * The most basic instances of `Job` interface are created like this:
 27â†’ *
 28â†’ * * **Coroutine job** is created with [launch][CoroutineScope.launch] coroutine builder.
 29â†’ *   It runs a specified block of code and completes on completion of this block.
 30â†’ * * **[CompletableJob]** is created with a `Job()` factory function.
 31â†’ *   It is completed by calling [CompletableJob.complete].
 32â†’ *
 33â†’ * Conceptually, an execution of a job does not produce a result value. Jobs are launched solely for their
 34â†’ * side effects. See [Deferred] interface for a job that produces a result.
 35â†’ *
 36â†’ * ### Job states
 37â†’ *
 38â†’ * A job has the following states:
 39â†’ *
 40â†’ * | **State**                        | [isActive] | [isCompleted] | [isCancelled] |
 41â†’ * | -------------------------------- | ---------- | ------------- | ------------- |
 42â†’ * | _New_ (optional initial state)   | `false`    | `false`       | `false`       |
 43â†’ * | _Active_ (default initial state) | `true`     | `false`       | `false`       |
 44â†’ * | _Completing_ (transient state)   | `true`     | `false`       | `false`       |
 45â†’ * | _Cancelling_ (transient state)   | `false`    | `false`       | `true`        |
 46â†’ * | _Cancelled_ (final state)        | `false`    | `true`        | `true`        |
 47â†’ * | _Completed_ (final state)        | `false`    | `true`        | `false`       |
 48â†’ *
 49â†’ * Usually, a job is created in the _active_ state (it is created and started). However, coroutine builders
 50â†’ * that provide an optional `start` parameter create a coroutine in the _new_ state when this parameter is set to
 51â†’ * [CoroutineStart.LAZY]. Such a job can be made _active_ by invoking [start] or [join].
 52â†’ *
 53â†’ * A job is _active_ while the coroutine is working or until [CompletableJob] is completed,
 54â†’ * or until it fails or cancelled.
 55â†’ *
 56â†’ * Failure of an _active_ job with an exception makes it _cancelling_.
 57â†’ * A job can be cancelled at any time with [cancel] function that forces it to transition to
 58â†’ * the _cancelling_ state immediately. The job becomes _cancelled_  when it finishes executing its work and
 59â†’ * all its children complete.
 60â†’ *
 61â†’ * Completion of an _active_ coroutine's body or a call to [CompletableJob.complete] transitions the job to
 62â†’ * the _completing_ state. It waits in the _completing_ state for all its children to complete before
 63â†’ * transitioning to the _completed_ state.
 64â†’ * Note that _completing_ state is purely internal to the job. For an outside observer a _completing_ job is still
 65â†’ * active, while internally it is waiting for its children.
 66â†’ *
 67â†’ * ```
 68â†’ *                                       wait children
 69â†’ * +-----+ start  +--------+ complete   +-------------+  finish  +-----------+
 70â†’ * | New | -----> | Active | ---------> | Completing  | -------> | Completed |
 71â†’ * +-----+        +--------+            +-------------+          +-----------+
 72â†’ *                  |  cancel / fail       |
 73â†’ *                  |     +----------------+
 74â†’ *                  |     |
 75â†’ *                  V     V
 76â†’ *              +------------+                           finish  +-----------+
 77â†’ *              | Cancelling | --------------------------------> | Cancelled |
 78â†’ *              +------------+                                   +-----------+
 79â†’ * ```
 80â†’ *
 81â†’ * A `Job` instance in the
 82â†’ * [coroutineContext](https://kotlinlang.org/api/latest/jvm/stdlib/kotlin.coroutines/coroutine-context.html)
 83â†’ * represents the coroutine itself.
 84â†’ *
 85â†’ * ### Cancellation cause
 86â†’ *
 87â†’ * A coroutine job is said to _complete exceptionally_ when its body throws an exception;
 88â†’ * a [CompletableJob] is completed exceptionally by calling [CompletableJob.completeExceptionally].
 89â†’ * An exceptionally completed job is cancelled and the corresponding exception becomes the _cancellation cause_ of the job.
 90â†’ *
 91â†’ * Normal cancellation of a job is distinguished from its failure by the type of this exception that caused its cancellation.
 92â†’ * A coroutine that threw [CancellationException] is considered to be _cancelled normally_.
 93â†’ * If a cancellation cause is a different exception type, then the job is considered to have _failed_.
 94â†’ * When a job has _failed_, then its parent gets cancelled with the exception of the same type,
 95â†’ * thus ensuring transparency in delegating parts of the job to its children.
 96â†’ *
 97â†’ * Note, that [cancel] function on a job only accepts [CancellationException] as a cancellation cause, thus
 98â†’ * calling [cancel] always results in a normal cancellation of a job, which does not lead to cancellation
 99â†’ * of its parent. This way, a parent can [cancel] its own children (cancelling all their children recursively, too)
100â†’ * without cancelling itself.
101â†’ *
102â†’ * ### Concurrency and synchronization
103â†’ *
104â†’ * All functions on this interface and on all interfaces derived from it are **thread-safe** and can
105â†’ * be safely invoked from concurrent coroutines without external synchronization.
106â†’ *
107â†’ * ### Not stable for inheritance
108â†’ *
109â†’ * **`Job` interface and all its derived interfaces are not stable for inheritance in 3rd party libraries**,
110â†’ * as new methods might be added to this interface in the future, but is stable for use.
111â†’ */

1â†’<!--- TEST_NAME DispatcherGuideTest -->
  2â†’
  3â†’[//]: # (title: Coroutine context and dispatchers)
  4â†’
  5â†’Coroutines always execute in some context represented by a value of the 
  6â†’[CoroutineContext](https://kotlinlang.org/api/latest/jvm/stdlib/kotlin.coroutines/-coroutine-context/) 
  7â†’type, defined in the Kotlin standard library.
  8â†’
  9â†’The coroutine context is a set of various elements. The main elements are the [Job] of the coroutine, 
 10â†’which we've seen before, and its dispatcher, which is covered in this section.
 11â†’
 12â†’## Dispatchers and threads
 13â†’
 14â†’The coroutine context includes a _coroutine dispatcher_ (see [CoroutineDispatcher]) that determines what thread or threads 
 15â†’the corresponding coroutine uses for its execution. The coroutine dispatcher can confine coroutine execution 
 16â†’to a specific thread, dispatch it to a thread pool, or let it run unconfined. 
 17â†’
 18â†’All coroutine builders like [launch] and [async] accept an optional 
 19â†’[CoroutineContext](https://kotlinlang.org/api/latest/jvm/stdlib/kotlin.coroutines/-coroutine-context/) 
 20â†’parameter that can be used to explicitly specify the dispatcher for the new coroutine and other context elements. 
 21â†’
 22â†’Try the following example:
 23â†’
 24â†’```kotlin
 25â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
 26â†’
 27â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
 28â†’//sampleStart
 29â†’    launch { // context of the parent, main runBlocking coroutine
 30â†’        println("main runBlocking      : I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 31â†’    }
 32â†’    launch(Dispatchers.Unconfined) { // not confined -- will work with main thread
 33â†’        println("Unconfined            : I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 34â†’    }
 35â†’    launch(Dispatchers.Default) { // will get dispatched to DefaultDispatcher 
 36â†’        println("Default               : I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 37â†’    }
 38â†’    launch(newSingleThreadContext("MyOwnThread")) { // will get its own new thread
 39â†’        println("newSingleThreadContext: I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 40â†’    }
 41â†’//sampleEnd    
 42â†’}
 43â†’```
 44â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
 45â†’
 46â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-01.kt).
 47â†’>
 48â†’{type="note"}
 49â†’
 50â†’It produces the following output (maybe in different order):
 51â†’
 52â†’```text
 53â†’Unconfined            : I'm working in thread main
 54â†’Default               : I'm working in thread DefaultDispatcher-worker-1
 55â†’newSingleThreadContext: I'm working in thread MyOwnThread
 56â†’main runBlocking      : I'm working in thread main
 57â†’```
 58â†’
 59â†’<!--- TEST LINES_START_UNORDERED -->
 60â†’
 61â†’When `launch { ... }` is used without parameters, it inherits the context (and thus dispatcher)
 62â†’from the [CoroutineScope] it is being launched from. In this case, it inherits the
 63â†’context of the main `runBlocking` coroutine which runs in the `main` thread. 
 64â†’
 65â†’[Dispatchers.Unconfined] is a special dispatcher that also appears to run in the `main` thread, but it is,
 66â†’in fact, a different mechanism that is explained later.
 67â†’
 68â†’The default dispatcher is used when no other dispatcher is explicitly specified in the scope.
 69â†’It is represented by [Dispatchers.Default] and uses a shared background pool of threads.
 70â†’  
 71â†’[newSingleThreadContext] creates a thread for the coroutine to run. 
 72â†’A dedicated thread is a very expensive resource. 
 73â†’In a real application it must be either released, when no longer needed, using the [close][ExecutorCoroutineDispatcher.close] 
 74â†’function, or stored in a top-level variable and reused throughout the application.  
 75â†’
 76â†’## Unconfined vs confined dispatcher
 77â†’ 
 78â†’The [Dispatchers.Unconfined] coroutine dispatcher starts a coroutine in the caller thread, but only until the
 79â†’first suspension point. After suspension it resumes the coroutine in the thread that is fully determined by the
 80â†’suspending function that was invoked. The unconfined dispatcher is appropriate for coroutines which neither
 81â†’consume CPU time nor update any shared data (like UI) confined to a specific thread. 
 82â†’
 83â†’On the other side, the dispatcher is inherited from the outer [CoroutineScope] by default. 
 84â†’The default dispatcher for the [runBlocking] coroutine, in particular,
 85â†’is confined to the invoker thread, so inheriting it has the effect of confining execution to
 86â†’this thread with predictable FIFO scheduling.
 87â†’
 88â†’```kotlin
 89â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
 90â†’
 91â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
 92â†’//sampleStart
 93â†’    launch(Dispatchers.Unconfined) { // not confined -- will work with main thread
 94â†’        println("Unconfined      : I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 95â†’        delay(500)
 96â†’        println("Unconfined      : After delay in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 97â†’    }
 98â†’    launch { // context of the parent, main runBlocking coroutine
 99â†’        println("main runBlocking: I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
100â†’        delay(1000)
101â†’        println("main runBlocking: After delay in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
102â†’    }
103â†’//sampleEnd    
104â†’}
105â†’```
106â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
107â†’
108â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-02.kt).
109â†’>
110â†’{type="note"}
111â†’
112â†’Produces the output: 
113â†’ 
114â†’```text
115â†’Unconfined      : I'm working in thread main
116â†’main runBlocking: I'm working in thread main
117â†’Unconfined      : After delay in thread kotlinx.coroutines.DefaultExecutor
118â†’main runBlocking: After delay in thread main
119â†’```
120â†’
121â†’<!--- TEST LINES_START -->
122â†’ 
123â†’So, the coroutine with the context inherited from `runBlocking {...}` continues to execute
124â†’in the `main` thread, while the unconfined one resumes in the default executor thread that the [delay]
125â†’function is using.
126â†’
127â†’> The unconfined dispatcher is an advanced mechanism that can be helpful in certain corner cases where
128â†’> dispatching of a coroutine for its execution later is not needed or produces undesirable side-effects,
129â†’> because some operation in a coroutine must be performed right away. 
130â†’> The unconfined dispatcher should not be used in general code. 
131â†’>
132â†’{type="note"}
133â†’
134â†’## Debugging coroutines and threads
135â†’
136â†’Coroutines can suspend on one thread and resume on another thread. 
137â†’Even with a single-threaded dispatcher it might be hard to
138â†’figure out what the coroutine was doing, where, and when if you don't have special tooling. 
139â†’
140â†’### Debugging with IDEA
141â†’
142â†’The Coroutine Debugger of the Kotlin plugin simplifies debugging coroutines in IntelliJ IDEA.
143â†’
144â†’> Debugging works for versions 1.3.8 or later of `kotlinx-coroutines-core`.
145â†’>
146â†’{type="note"}
147â†’
148â†’The **Debug** tool window contains the **Coroutines** tab. In this tab, you can find information about both currently running and suspended coroutines. 
149â†’The coroutines are grouped by the dispatcher they are running on.
150â†’
151â†’![Debugging coroutines](coroutine-idea-debugging-1.png){width=700}
152â†’
153â†’With the coroutine debugger, you can:
154â†’* Check the state of each coroutine.
155â†’* See the values of local and captured variables for both running and suspended coroutines.
156â†’* See a full coroutine creation stack, as well as a call stack inside the coroutine. The stack includes all frames with 
157â†’variable values, even those that would be lost during standard debugging.
158â†’* Get a full report that contains the state of each coroutine and its stack. To obtain it, right-click inside the **Coroutines** tab, and then click **Get Coroutines Dump**.
159â†’
160â†’To start coroutine debugging, you just need to set breakpoints and run the application in debug mode.
161â†’
162â†’Learn more about coroutines debugging in the [tutorial](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/tutorials/coroutines/debug-coroutines-with-idea.html).
163â†’
164â†’### Debugging using logging
165â†’
166â†’Another approach to debugging applications with 
167â†’threads without Coroutine Debugger is to print the thread name in the log file on each log statement. This feature is universally supported
168â†’by logging frameworks. When using coroutines, the thread name alone does not give much of a context, so 
169â†’`kotlinx.coroutines` includes debugging facilities to make it easier. 
170â†’
171â†’Run the following code with `-Dkotlinx.coroutines.debug` JVM option:
172â†’
173â†’```kotlin
174â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
175â†’
176â†’fun log(msg: String) = println("[${Thread.currentThread().name}] $msg")
177â†’
178â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
179â†’//sampleStart
180â†’    val a = async {
181â†’        log("I'm computing a piece of the answer")
182â†’        6
183â†’    }
184â†’    val b = async {
185â†’        log("I'm computing another piece of the answer")
186â†’        7
187â†’    }
188â†’    log("The answer is ${a.await() * b.await()}")
189â†’//sampleEnd    
190â†’}
191â†’```
192â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
193â†’
194â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-03.kt).
195â†’>
196â†’{type="note"}
197â†’
198â†’There are three coroutines. The main coroutine (#1) inside `runBlocking` 
199â†’and two coroutines computing the deferred values `a` (#2) and `b` (#3).
200â†’They are all executing in the context of `runBlocking` and are confined to the main thread.
201â†’The output of this code is:
202â†’
203â†’```text
204â†’[main @coroutine#2] I'm computing a piece of the answer
205â†’[main @coroutine#3] I'm computing another piece of the answer
206â†’[main @coroutine#1] The answer is 42
207â†’```
208â†’
209â†’<!--- TEST FLEXIBLE_THREAD -->
210â†’
211â†’The `log` function prints the name of the thread in square brackets, and you can see that it is the `main`
212â†’thread with the identifier of the currently executing coroutine appended to it. This identifier 
213â†’is consecutively assigned to all created coroutines when the debugging mode is on.
214â†’
215â†’> Debugging mode is also turned on when JVM is run with `-ea` option.
216â†’> You can read more about debugging facilities in the documentation of the [DEBUG_PROPERTY_NAME] property.
217â†’>
218â†’{type="note"}

220â†’## Jumping between threads
221â†’
222â†’Run the following code with the `-Dkotlinx.coroutines.debug` JVM option (see [debug](#debugging-coroutines-and-threads)):
223â†’
224â†’```kotlin
225â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
226â†’
227â†’fun log(msg: String) = println("[${Thread.currentThread().name}] $msg")
228â†’
229â†’fun main() {
230â†’//sampleStart
231â†’    newSingleThreadContext("Ctx1").use { ctx1 ->
232â†’        newSingleThreadContext("Ctx2").use { ctx2 ->
233â†’            runBlocking(ctx1) {
234â†’                log("Started in ctx1")
235â†’                withContext(ctx2) {
236â†’                    log("Working in ctx2")
237â†’                }
238â†’                log("Back to ctx1")
239â†’            }
240â†’        }
241â†’    }
242â†’//sampleEnd    
243â†’}
244â†’```
245â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
246â†’
247â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-04.kt).
248â†’>
249â†’{type="note"}
250â†’
251â†’It demonstrates several new techniques. One is using [runBlocking] with an explicitly specified context, and
252â†’the other one is using the [withContext] function to change the context of a coroutine while still staying in the
253â†’same coroutine, as you can see in the output below:
254â†’
255â†’```text
256â†’[Ctx1 @coroutine#1] Started in ctx1
257â†’[Ctx2 @coroutine#1] Working in ctx2
258â†’[Ctx1 @coroutine#1] Back to ctx1
259â†’```
260â†’
261â†’<!--- TEST -->

263â†’Note that this example also uses the `use` function from the Kotlin standard library to release threads
264â†’created with [newSingleThreadContext] when they are no longer needed.

266â†’## Job in the context
267â†’
268â†’The coroutine's [Job] is part of its context, and can be retrieved from it 
269â†’using the `coroutineContext[Job]` expression:
270â†’
271â†’```kotlin
272â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
273â†’
274â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
275â†’//sampleStart
276â†’    println("My job is ${coroutineContext[Job]}")
277â†’//sampleEnd    
278â†’}
279â†’```

282â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-05.kt).
283â†’>

285â†’
286â†’In the [debug mode](#debugging-coroutines-and-threads), it outputs something like this:
287â†’
288â†’```
289â†’My job is "coroutine#1":BlockingCoroutine{Active}@6d311334
290â†’```
291â†’
292â†’<!--- TEST lines.size == 1 && lines[0].startsWith("My job is \"coroutine#1\":BlockingCoroutine{Active}@") -->

294â†’Note that [isActive] in [CoroutineScope] is just a convenient shortcut for
295â†’`coroutineContext[Job]?.isActive == true`.

297â†’## Children of a coroutine
298â†’
299â†’When a coroutine is launched in the [CoroutineScope] of another coroutine,
300â†’it inherits its context via [CoroutineScope.coroutineContext] and 
301â†’the [Job] of the new coroutine becomes
302â†’a _child_ of the parent coroutine's job. When the parent coroutine is cancelled, all its children
303â†’are recursively cancelled, too. 
304â†’
305â†’However, this parent-child relation can be explicitly overriden in one of two ways:
306â†’
307â†’1. When a different scope is explicitly specified when launching a coroutine (for example, `GlobalScope.launch`), 
308â†’   then it does not inherit a `Job` from the parent scope.
309â†’2. When a different `Job` object is passed as the context for the new coroutine (as shown in the example below),
310â†’   then it overrides the `Job` of the parent scope.
311â†’   
312â†’In both cases, the launched coroutine is not tied to the scope it was launched from and operates independently.
313â†’
314â†’```kotlin
315â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
316â†’
317â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
318â†’//sampleStart
319â†’    // launch a coroutine to process some kind of incoming request
320â†’    val request = launch {
321â†’        // it spawns two other jobs
322â†’        launch(Job()) { 
323â†’            println("job1: I run in my own Job and execute independently!")
324â†’            delay(1000)
325â†’            println("job1: I am not affected by cancellation of the request")
326â†’        }
327â†’        // and the other inherits the parent context
328â†’        launch {
329â†’            delay(100)
330â†’            println("job2: I am a child of the request coroutine")
331â†’            delay(1000)
332â†’            println("job2: I will not execute this line if my parent request is cancelled")
333â†’        }
334â†’    }
335â†’    delay(500)
336â†’    request.cancel() // cancel processing of the request
337â†’    println("main: Who has survived request cancellation?")
338â†’    delay(1000) // delay the main thread for a second to see what happens
339â†’//sampleEnd
340â†’}
341â†’```
342â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
343â†’
344â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-06.kt).
345â†’>
346â†’{type="note"}
347â†’
348â†’The output of this code is:
349â†’
350â†’```text
351â†’job1: I run in my own Job and execute independently!
352â†’job2: I am a child of the request coroutine
353â†’main: Who has survived request cancellation?
354â†’job1: I am not affected by cancellation of the request
355â†’```
356â†’

359â†’## Parental responsibilities 
360â†’
361â†’A parent coroutine always waits for completion of all its children. A parent does not have to explicitly track
362â†’all the children it launches, and it does not have to use [Job.join] to wait for them at the end:
363â†’
364â†’```kotlin
365â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
366â†’
367â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
368â†’//sampleStart
369â†’    // launch a coroutine to process some kind of incoming request
370â†’    val request = launch {
371â†’        repeat(3) { i -> // launch a few children jobs
372â†’            launch  {
373â†’                delay((i + 1) * 200L) // variable delay 200ms, 400ms, 600ms
374â†’                println("Coroutine $i is done")
375â†’            }
376â†’        }
377â†’        println("request: I'm done and I don't explicitly join my children that are still active")
378â†’    }
379â†’    request.join() // wait for completion of the request, including all its children
380â†’    println("Now processing of the request is complete")
381â†’//sampleEnd
382â†’}
383â†’```
384â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
385â†’
386â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-07.kt).
387â†’>
388â†’{type="note"}
389â†’
390â†’The result is going to be:
391â†’
392â†’```text
393â†’request: I'm done and I don't explicitly join my children that are still active
394â†’Coroutine 0 is done
395â†’Coroutine 1 is done
396â†’Coroutine 2 is done
397â†’Now processing of the request is complete
398â†’```
399â†’
400â†’<!--- TEST -->
401â†’
402â†’## Naming coroutines for debugging
403â†’
404â†’Automatically assigned ids are good when coroutines log often and you just need to correlate log records
405â†’coming from the same coroutine. However, when a coroutine is tied to the processing of a specific request
406â†’or doing some specific background task, it is better to name it explicitly for debugging purposes.
407â†’The [CoroutineName] context element serves the same purpose as the thread name. It is included in the thread name that
408â†’is executing this coroutine when the [debugging mode](#debugging-coroutines-and-threads) is turned on.
409â†’
410â†’The following example demonstrates this concept:
411â†’
412â†’```kotlin
413â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
414â†’
415â†’fun log(msg: String) = println("[${Thread.currentThread().name}] $msg")
416â†’
417â†’fun main() = runBlocking(CoroutineName("main")) {
418â†’//sampleStart
419â†’    log("Started main coroutine")
420â†’    // run two background value computations
421â†’    val v1 = async(CoroutineName("v1coroutine")) {
422â†’        delay(500)
423â†’        log("Computing v1")
424â†’        252
425â†’    }
426â†’    val v2 = async(CoroutineName("v2coroutine")) {
427â†’        delay(1000)
428â†’        log("Computing v2")
429â†’        6
430â†’    }
431â†’    log("The answer for v1 / v2 = ${v1.await() / v2.await()}")
432â†’//sampleEnd    
433â†’}
434â†’```
435â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
436â†’
437â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-08.kt).
438â†’>
439â†’{type="note"}
440â†’
441â†’The output it produces with `-Dkotlinx.coroutines.debug` JVM option is similar to:
442â†’ 
443â†’```text
444â†’[main @main#1] Started main coroutine
445â†’[main @v1coroutine#2] Computing v1
446â†’[main @v2coroutine#3] Computing v2
447â†’[main @main#1] The answer for v1 / v2 = 42
448â†’```

452â†’## Combining context elements
453â†’
454â†’Sometimes we need to define multiple elements for a coroutine context. We can use the `+` operator for that.
455â†’For example, we can launch a coroutine with an explicitly specified dispatcher and an explicitly specified 
456â†’name at the same time: 
457â†’
458â†’```kotlin
459â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
460â†’
461â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
462â†’//sampleStart
463â†’    launch(Dispatchers.Default + CoroutineName("test")) {
464â†’        println("I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
465â†’    }
466â†’//sampleEnd    
467â†’}
468â†’```
469â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}

472â†’>

474â†’
475â†’The output of this code with the `-Dkotlinx.coroutines.debug` JVM option is: 
476â†’

478â†’I'm working in thread DefaultDispatcher-worker-1 @test#2
479â†’```

483â†’## Coroutine scope
484â†’
485â†’Let us put our knowledge about contexts, children and jobs together. Assume that our application has
486â†’an object with a lifecycle, but that object is not a coroutine. For example, we are writing an Android application
487â†’and launch various coroutines in the context of an Android activity to perform asynchronous operations to fetch 
488â†’and update data, do animations, etc. All of these coroutines must be cancelled when the activity is destroyed
489â†’to avoid memory leaks. We, of course, can manipulate contexts and jobs manually to tie the lifecycles of the activity 
490â†’and its coroutines, but `kotlinx.coroutines` provides an abstraction encapsulating that: [CoroutineScope].
491â†’You should be already familiar with the coroutine scope as all coroutine builders are declared as extensions on it. 
492â†’
493â†’We manage the lifecycles of our coroutines by creating an instance of [CoroutineScope] tied to 
494â†’the lifecycle of our activity. A `CoroutineScope` instance can be created by the [CoroutineScope()] or [MainScope()]
495â†’factory functions. The former creates a general-purpose scope, while the latter creates a scope for UI applications and uses
496â†’[Dispatchers.Main] as the default dispatcher:
497â†’
498â†’```kotlin
499â†’class Activity {
500â†’    private val mainScope = MainScope()
501â†’    
502â†’    fun destroy() {
503â†’        mainScope.cancel()
504â†’    }
505â†’    // to be continued ...
506â†’```
507â†’
508â†’Now, we can launch coroutines in the scope of this `Activity` using the defined `scope`.
509â†’For the demo, we launch ten coroutines that delay for a different time:
510â†’
511â†’```kotlin
512â†’    // class Activity continues
513â†’    fun doSomething() {
514â†’        // launch ten coroutines for a demo, each working for a different time
515â†’        repeat(10) { i ->
516â†’            mainScope.launch {
517â†’                delay((i + 1) * 200L) // variable delay 200ms, 400ms, ... etc
518â†’                println("Coroutine $i is done")
519â†’            }
520â†’        }
521â†’    }
522â†’} // class Activity ends
523â†’``` 
524â†’
525â†’In our main function we create the activity, call our test `doSomething` function, and destroy the activity after 500ms.
526â†’This cancels all the coroutines that were launched from `doSomething`. We can see that because after the destruction 
527â†’of the activity no more messages are printed, even if we wait a little longer.
528â†’
529â†’<!--- CLEAR -->
530â†’
531â†’```kotlin
532â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
533â†’
534â†’class Activity {
535â†’    private val mainScope = CoroutineScope(Dispatchers.Default) // use Default for test purposes
536â†’    
537â†’    fun destroy() {
538â†’        mainScope.cancel()
539â†’    }
540â†’
541â†’    fun doSomething() {
542â†’        // launch ten coroutines for a demo, each working for a different time
543â†’        repeat(10) { i ->
544â†’            mainScope.launch {
545â†’                delay((i + 1) * 200L) // variable delay 200ms, 400ms, ... etc
546â†’                println("Coroutine $i is done")
547â†’            }
548â†’        }
549â†’    }
550â†’} // class Activity ends
551â†’
552â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
553â†’//sampleStart
554â†’    val activity = Activity()
555â†’    activity.doSomething() // run test function
556â†’    println("Launched coroutines")
557â†’    delay(500L) // delay for half a second
558â†’    println("Destroying activity!")
559â†’    activity.destroy() // cancels all coroutines
560â†’    delay(1000) // visually confirm that they don't work
561â†’//sampleEnd    
562â†’}
563â†’```
564â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
565â†’
566â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-10.kt).
567â†’>
568â†’{type="note"}
569â†’
570â†’The output of this example is:
571â†’
572â†’```text
573â†’Launched coroutines
574â†’Coroutine 0 is done
575â†’Coroutine 1 is done
576â†’Destroying activity!
577â†’```
578â†’
579â†’<!--- TEST -->
580â†’
581â†’As you can see, only the first two coroutines print a message and the others are cancelled 
582â†’by a single invocation of `job.cancel()` in `Activity.destroy()`.
583â†’
584â†’> Note, that Android has first-party support for coroutine scope in all entities with the lifecycle.
585â†’> See [the corresponding documentation](https://developer.android.com/topic/libraries/architecture/coroutines#lifecyclescope).
586â†’>
587â†’{type="note"}
588â†’
589â†’### Thread-local data
590â†’
591â†’Sometimes it is convenient to have an ability to pass some thread-local data to or between coroutines. 
592â†’However, since they are not bound to any particular thread, this will likely lead to boilerplate if done manually.
593â†’
594â†’For [`ThreadLocal`](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/lang/ThreadLocal.html), 
595â†’the [asContextElement] extension function is here for the rescue. It creates an additional context element 
596â†’which keeps the value of the given `ThreadLocal` and restores it every time the coroutine switches its context.
597â†’
598â†’It is easy to demonstrate it in action:
599â†’
600â†’```kotlin
601â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
602â†’
603â†’val threadLocal = ThreadLocal<String?>() // declare thread-local variable
604â†’
605â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {

625â†’In this example we launch a new coroutine in a background thread pool using [Dispatchers.Default], so
626â†’it works on a different thread from the thread pool, but it still has the value of the thread local variable
627â†’that we specified using `threadLocal.asContextElement(value = "launch")`,
628â†’no matter which thread the coroutine is executed on.

632â†’Pre-main, current thread: Thread[main @coroutine#1,5,main], thread local value: 'main'
633â†’Launch start, current thread: Thread[DefaultDispatcher-worker-1 @coroutine#2,5,main], thread local value: 'launch'
634â†’After yield, current thread: Thread[DefaultDispatcher-worker-2 @coroutine#2,5,main], thread local value: 'launch'

640â†’It's easy to forget to set the corresponding context element. The thread-local variable accessed from the coroutine may
641â†’then have an unexpected value, if the thread running the coroutine is different.
642â†’To avoid such situations, it is recommended to use the [ensurePresent] method
643â†’and fail-fast on improper usages.
644â†’
645â†’`ThreadLocal` has first-class support and can be used with any primitive `kotlinx.coroutines` provides.
646â†’It has one key limitation, though: when a thread-local is mutated, a new value is not propagated to the coroutine caller 
647â†’(because a context element cannot track all `ThreadLocal` object accesses), and the updated value is lost on the next suspension.
648â†’Use [withContext] to update the value of the thread-local in a coroutine, see [asContextElement] for more details. 
649â†’
650â†’Alternatively, a value can be stored in a mutable box like `class Counter(var i: Int)`, which is, in turn, 
651â†’stored in a thread-local variable. However, in this case you are fully responsible to synchronize 
652â†’potentially concurrent modifications to the variable in this mutable box.
653â†’
654â†’For advanced usage, for example for integration with logging MDC, transactional contexts or any other libraries
655â†’which internally use thread-locals for passing data, see the documentation of the [ThreadContextElement] interface

- **Conceptual correctness**: If there's no Job, it hasn't been completed or cancelled, so it should be considered active
- **Fixes real issues**: Solves problems with non-kotlinx.coroutines entry points like Ktor and `suspend fun main`

Perfect! Let me create a final summary of the implementation:

## Summary

I have successfully implemented a fix for the issue "Consider deprecating or changing the behaviour of CoroutineContext.isActive" by implementing **Option 3: Change the default behaviour to return `true`**.

### Problem Statement

The original `CoroutineContext.isActive` returned `false` when there was no Job in the context (using `this[Job]?.isActive == true`). This was error-prone for non-kotlinx.coroutines entry points like Ktor and `suspend fun main` where a Job might not be present, as it was inconsistent with the contract that a context without a Job hasn't been completed or cancelled.

### Solution

Changed `CoroutineContext.isActive` to return `true` when there's no Job, aligning it with `CoroutineScope.isActive`.

### Changes Made

1. **`/project/kotlinx-coroutines-core/common/src/Job.kt`** (lines 556-557)
   - Changed implementation from: `this[Job]?.isActive == true`
   - To: `this[Job]?.isActive ?: true`
   - Updated documentation to reflect the new behavior

2. **`/project/docs/topics/coroutine-context-and-dispatchers.md`** (line 295)
   - Fixed documentation to accurately describe the behavior: `coroutineContext[Job]?.isActive ?: true`

3. **`/project/kotlinx-coroutines-core/common/test/CoroutineContextIsActiveTest.kt`** (new file)
   - Created comprehensive test suite with 10 test cases covering:
     - Contexts with active, cancelled, and completed Jobs
     - Contexts without Jobs (should return `true`)
     - Empty contexts (should return `true`)
     - Consistency with `CoroutineScope.isActive`
     - The original issue scenario (suspend fun main / Ktor entry points)
     - GlobalScope and NonCancellable contexts

### Benefits

1. **Consistency**: `CoroutineContext.isActive` now behaves identically to `CoroutineScope.isActive`
2. **Conceptual correctness**: A context without a Job hasn't been completed or cancelled, so it's logically active
3. **Fixes real issues**: Solves problems with non-kotlinx.coroutines entry points
4. **Minimal breaking changes**: As discussed in the issue, this will "fix more things than break them"

### Technical Details

The fix ensures that code patterns like:
```kotlin
while (coroutineContext.isActive) {
    // do some computation
}
```
work correctly in all contexts, including those without a Job (like suspend fun main or Ktor handlers), rather than immediately returning false and breaking the loop unexpectedly.

 13â†’import kotlin.jvm.*
 14â†’
 15â†’// --------------- core job interfaces ---------------
 16â†’
 17â†’/**
 18â†’ * A background job. Conceptually, a job is a cancellable thing with a life-cycle that
 19â†’ * culminates in its completion.
 20â†’ *
 21â†’ * Jobs can be arranged into parent-child hierarchies where cancellation
 22â†’ * of a parent leads to immediate cancellation of all its [children] recursively.
 23â†’ * Failure of a child with an exception other than [CancellationException] immediately cancels its parent and,
 24â†’ * consequently, all its other children. This behavior can be customized using [SupervisorJob].
 25â†’ *
 26â†’ * The most basic instances of `Job` interface are created like this:
 27â†’ *
 28â†’ * * **Coroutine job** is created with [launch][CoroutineScope.launch] coroutine builder.
 29â†’ *   It runs a specified block of code and completes on completion of this block.
 30â†’ * * **[CompletableJob]** is created with a `Job()` factory function.
 31â†’ *   It is completed by calling [CompletableJob.complete].
 32â†’ *
 33â†’ * Conceptually, an execution of a job does not produce a result value. Jobs are launched solely for their
 34â†’ * side effects. See [Deferred] interface for a job that produces a result.
 35â†’ *
 36â†’ * ### Job states
 37â†’ *
 38â†’ * A job has the following states:
 39â†’ *
 40â†’ * | **State**                        | [isActive] | [isCompleted] | [isCancelled] |
 41â†’ * | -------------------------------- | ---------- | ------------- | ------------- |
 42â†’ * | _New_ (optional initial state)   | `false`    | `false`       | `false`       |
 43â†’ * | _Active_ (default initial state) | `true`     | `false`       | `false`       |
 44â†’ * | _Completing_ (transient state)   | `true`     | `false`       | `false`       |
 45â†’ * | _Cancelling_ (transient state)   | `false`    | `false`       | `true`        |
 46â†’ * | _Cancelled_ (final state)        | `false`    | `true`        | `true`        |
 47â†’ * | _Completed_ (final state)        | `false`    | `true`        | `false`       |
 48â†’ *
 49â†’ * Usually, a job is created in the _active_ state (it is created and started). However, coroutine builders
 50â†’ * that provide an optional `start` parameter create a coroutine in the _new_ state when this parameter is set to
 51â†’ * [CoroutineStart.LAZY]. Such a job can be made _active_ by invoking [start] or [join].
 52â†’ *
 53â†’ * A job is _active_ while the coroutine is working or until [CompletableJob] is completed,
 54â†’ * or until it fails or cancelled.
 55â†’ *
 56â†’ * Failure of an _active_ job with an exception makes it _cancelling_.
 57â†’ * A job can be cancelled at any time with [cancel] function that forces it to transition to
 58â†’ * the _cancelling_ state immediately. The job becomes _cancelled_  when it finishes executing its work and
 59â†’ * all its children complete.
 60â†’ *
 61â†’ * Completion of an _active_ coroutine's body or a call to [CompletableJob.complete] transitions the job to
 62â†’ * the _completing_ state. It waits in the _completing_ state for all its children to complete before
 63â†’ * transitioning to the _completed_ state.
 64â†’ * Note that _completing_ state is purely internal to the job. For an outside observer a _completing_ job is still
 65â†’ * active, while internally it is waiting for its children.
 66â†’ *
 67â†’ * ```
 68â†’ *                                       wait children
 69â†’ * +-----+ start  +--------+ complete   +-------------+  finish  +-----------+
 70â†’ * | New | -----> | Active | ---------> | Completing  | -------> | Completed |
 71â†’ * +-----+        +--------+            +-------------+          +-----------+
 72â†’ *                  |  cancel / fail       |
 73â†’ *                  |     +----------------+
 74â†’ *                  |     |
 75â†’ *                  V     V
 76â†’ *              +------------+                           finish  +-----------+
 77â†’ *              | Cancelling | --------------------------------> | Cancelled |
 78â†’ *              +------------+                                   +-----------+
 79â†’ * ```
 80â†’ *
 81â†’ * A `Job` instance in the
 82â†’ * [coroutineContext](https://kotlinlang.org/api/latest/jvm/stdlib/kotlin.coroutines/coroutine-context.html)
 83â†’ * represents the coroutine itself.
 84â†’ *
 85â†’ * ### Cancellation cause
 86â†’ *
 87â†’ * A coroutine job is said to _complete exceptionally_ when its body throws an exception;
 88â†’ * a [CompletableJob] is completed exceptionally by calling [CompletableJob.completeExceptionally].
 89â†’ * An exceptionally completed job is cancelled and the corresponding exception becomes the _cancellation cause_ of the job.
 90â†’ *
 91â†’ * Normal cancellation of a job is distinguished from its failure by the type of this exception that caused its cancellation.
 92â†’ * A coroutine that threw [CancellationException] is considered to be _cancelled normally_.
 93â†’ * If a cancellation cause is a different exception type, then the job is considered to have _failed_.
 94â†’ * When a job has _failed_, then its parent gets cancelled with the exception of the same type,
 95â†’ * thus ensuring transparency in delegating parts of the job to its children.
 96â†’ *
 97â†’ * Note, that [cancel] function on a job only accepts [CancellationException] as a cancellation cause, thus
 98â†’ * calling [cancel] always results in a normal cancellation of a job, which does not lead to cancellation
 99â†’ * of its parent. This way, a parent can [cancel] its own children (cancelling all their children recursively, too)
100â†’ * without cancelling itself.
101â†’ *
102â†’ * ### Concurrency and synchronization
103â†’ *
104â†’ * All functions on this interface and on all interfaces derived from it are **thread-safe** and can
105â†’ * be safely invoked from concurrent coroutines without external synchronization.
106â†’ *
107â†’ * ### Not stable for inheritance
108â†’ *
109â†’ * **`Job` interface and all its derived interfaces are not stable for inheritance in 3rd party libraries**,
110â†’ * as new methods might be added to this interface in the future, but is stable for use.
111â†’ */

 14â†’/**
 15â†’ * Defines a scope for new coroutines. Every **coroutine builder** (like [launch], [async], etc.)
 16â†’ * is an extension on [CoroutineScope] and inherits its [coroutineContext][CoroutineScope.coroutineContext]
 17â†’ * to automatically propagate all its elements and cancellation.
 18â†’ *
 19â†’ * The best ways to obtain a standalone instance of the scope are [CoroutineScope()] and [MainScope()] factory functions,
 20â†’ * taking care to cancel these coroutine scopes when they are no longer needed (see section on custom usage below for
 21â†’ * explanation and example).
 22â†’ *
 23â†’ * Additional context elements can be appended to the scope using the [plus][CoroutineScope.plus] operator.
 24â†’ *
 25â†’ * ### Convention for structured concurrency
 26â†’ *
 27â†’ * Manual implementation of this interface is not recommended, implementation by delegation should be preferred instead.
 28â†’ * By convention, the [context of a scope][CoroutineScope.coroutineContext] should contain an instance of a
 29â†’ * [job][Job] to enforce the discipline of **structured concurrency** with propagation of cancellation.
 30â†’ *
 31â†’ * Every coroutine builder (like [launch], [async], and others)
 32â†’ * and every scoping function (like [coroutineScope] and [withContext]) provides _its own_ scope
 33â†’ * with its own [Job] instance into the inner block of code it runs.
 34â†’ * By convention, they all wait for all the coroutines inside their block to complete before completing themselves,
 35â†’ * thus enforcing the structured concurrency. See [Job] documentation for more details.
 36â†’ *
 37â†’ * ### Android usage
 38â†’ *

 45â†’ * responsible for launching child coroutines. The corresponding instance of `CoroutineScope` shall be created

 47â†’ *

 49â†’ *   and adds a [Job] if one is not provided as part of the context.
 50â†’ * * `MainScope()` uses [Dispatchers.Main] for its coroutines and has a [SupervisorJob].
 51â†’ *

 53â†’ * The [CoroutineScope.cancel] extension function shall be used when the entity that was launching coroutines
 54â†’ * is no longer needed. It cancels all the coroutines that might still be running on behalf of it.
 55â†’ *
 56â†’ * For example:
 57â†’ *
 58â†’ * ```

 64â†’ *         // ... do the rest of cleanup here ...
 65â†’ *     }

 67â†’ *     /*

 69â†’ *      * in this method throws an exception, then all nested coroutines are cancelled.
 70â†’ *      */
 71â†’ *     fun showSomeData() = scope.launch { // launched in the main thread
 72â†’ *        // ... here we can use suspending functions or coroutine builders with other dispatchers

 74â†’ *     }
 75â†’ * }
 76â†’ * ```
 77â†’ */

 80â†’     * The context of this scope.
 81â†’     * Context is encapsulated by the scope and used for implementation of coroutine builders that are extensions on the scope.
 82â†’     * Accessing this property in general code is not recommended for any purposes except accessing the [Job] instance for advanced usages.
 83â†’     *
 84â†’     * By convention, should contain an instance of a [job][Job] to enforce structured concurrency.

100â†’ *
101â†’ * Example of use:
102â†’ * ```
103â†’ * class MyAndroidActivity {
104â†’ *     private val scope = MainScope()
105â†’ *
106â†’ *     override fun onDestroy() {
107â†’ *         super.onDestroy()

115â†’ * `val scope = MainScope() + CoroutineName("MyActivity")`.

122â†’ *

124â†’ * ```

126â†’ *     // do some computation
127â†’ * }
128â†’ * ```
129â†’ *

131â†’ * [CoroutineScope] is available.

139â†’/**
140â†’ * A global [CoroutineScope] not bound to any job.
141â†’ * Global scope is used to launch top-level coroutines which are operating on the whole application lifetime
142â†’ * and are not cancelled prematurely.
143â†’ *
144â†’ * Active coroutines launched in `GlobalScope` do not keep the process alive. They are like daemon threads.
145â†’ *
146â†’ * This is a **delicate** API. It is easy to accidentally create resource or memory leaks when
147â†’ * `GlobalScope` is used. A coroutine launched in `GlobalScope` is not subject to the principle of structured
148â†’ * concurrency, so if it hangs or gets delayed due to a problem (e.g. due to a slow network), it will stay working
149â†’ * and consuming resources. For example, consider the following code:
150â†’ *
151â†’ * ```
152â†’ * fun loadConfiguration() {
153â†’ *     GlobalScope.launch {
154â†’ *         val config = fetchConfigFromServer() // network request
155â†’ *         updateConfiguration(config)
156â†’ *     }
157â†’ * }
158â†’ * ```
159â†’ *
160â†’ * A call to `loadConfiguration` creates a coroutine in the `GlobalScope` that works in background without any
161â†’ * provision to cancel it or to wait for its completion. If a network is slow, it keeps waiting in background,
162â†’ * consuming resources. Repeated calls to `loadConfiguration` will consume more and more resources.
163â†’ *
164â†’ * ### Possible replacements
165â†’ *
166â†’ * In many cases uses of `GlobalScope` should be removed, marking the containing operation with `suspend`, for example:
167â†’ *
168â†’ * ```
169â†’ * suspend fun loadConfiguration() {
170â†’ *     val config = fetchConfigFromServer() // network request
171â†’ *     updateConfiguration(config)
172â†’ * }
173â†’ * ```
174â†’ *
175â†’ * In cases when `GlobalScope.launch` was used to launch multiple concurrent operations, the corresponding
176â†’ * operations shall be grouped with [coroutineScope] instead:
177â†’ *
178â†’ * ```
179â†’ * // concurrently load configuration and data
180â†’ * suspend fun loadConfigurationAndData() {
181â†’ *     coroutineScope {
182â†’ *         launch { loadConfiguration() }
183â†’ *         launch { loadData() }
184â†’ *     }
185â†’ * }
186â†’ * ```
187â†’ *
188â†’ * In top-level code, when launching a concurrent operation from a non-suspending context, an appropriately
189â†’ * confined instance of [CoroutineScope] shall be used instead of a `GlobalScope`. See docs on [CoroutineScope] for
190â†’ * details.
191â†’ *
192â†’ * ### GlobalScope vs custom scope
193â†’ *
194â†’ * Do not replace `GlobalScope.launch { ... }` with `CoroutineScope().launch { ... }` constructor function call.
195â†’ * The latter has the same pitfalls as `GlobalScope`. See [CoroutineScope] documentation on the intended usage of
196â†’ * `CoroutineScope()` constructor function.
197â†’ *
198â†’ * ### Legitimate use-cases
199â†’ *
200â†’ * There are limited circumstances under which `GlobalScope` can be legitimately and safely used, such as top-level background
201â†’ * processes that must stay active for the whole duration of the application's lifetime. Because of that, any use
202â†’ * of `GlobalScope` requires an explicit opt-in with `@OptIn(DelicateCoroutinesApi::class)`, like this:
203â†’ *
204â†’ * ```
205â†’ * // A global coroutine to log statistics every second, must be always active
206â†’ * @OptIn(DelicateCoroutinesApi::class)
207â†’ * val globalScopeReporter = GlobalScope.launch {
208â†’ *     while (true) {
209â†’ *         delay(1000)
210â†’ *         logStatistics()
211â†’ *     }
212â†’ * }
213â†’ * ```

217â†’    /**
218â†’     * Returns [EmptyCoroutineContext].

227â†’ * the context's [Job].

229â†’ * This function is designed for _parallel decomposition_ of work. When any child coroutine in this scope fails,
230â†’ * this scope fails and all the rest of the children are cancelled (for a different behavior see [supervisorScope]).
231â†’ * This function returns as soon as the given block and all its children coroutines are completed.
232â†’ * A usage example of a scope looks like this:

236â†’ *     val data = async(Dispatchers.IO) { // <- extension on current scope
237â†’ *      ... load some UI data for the Main thread ...
238â†’ *     }
239â†’ *
240â†’ *     withContext(Dispatchers.Main) {
241â†’ *         doSomeWork()
242â†’ *         val result = data.await()
243â†’ *         display(result)
244â†’ *     }
245â†’ * }
246â†’ * ```

249â†’ * 1) `showSomeData` returns as soon as the data is loaded and displayed in the UI.
250â†’ * 2) If `doSomeWork` throws an exception, then the `async` task is cancelled and `showSomeData` rethrows that exception.
251â†’ * 3) If the outer scope of `showSomeData` is cancelled, both started `async` and `withContext` blocks are cancelled.
252â†’ * 4) If the `async` block fails, `withContext` will be cancelled.

255â†’ * or may throw a corresponding unhandled [Throwable] if there is any unhandled exception in this scope
256â†’ * (for example, from a crashed coroutine that was started with [launch][CoroutineScope.launch] in this scope).

262â†’    return suspendCoroutineUninterceptedOrReturn { uCont ->
263â†’        val coroutine = ScopeCoroutine(uCont.context, uCont)
264â†’        coroutine.startUndispatchedOrReturn(coroutine, block)

  3â†’[//]: # (title: Coroutine context and dispatchers)
  4â†’
  5â†’Coroutines always execute in some context represented by a value of the 
  6â†’[CoroutineContext](https://kotlinlang.org/api/latest/jvm/stdlib/kotlin.coroutines/-coroutine-context/) 
  7â†’type, defined in the Kotlin standard library.
  8â†’
  9â†’The coroutine context is a set of various elements. The main elements are the [Job] of the coroutine, 
 10â†’which we've seen before, and its dispatcher, which is covered in this section.
 11â†’
 12â†’## Dispatchers and threads
 13â†’
 14â†’The coroutine context includes a _coroutine dispatcher_ (see [CoroutineDispatcher]) that determines what thread or threads 
 15â†’the corresponding coroutine uses for its execution. The coroutine dispatcher can confine coroutine execution 
 16â†’to a specific thread, dispatch it to a thread pool, or let it run unconfined. 
 17â†’
 18â†’All coroutine builders like [launch] and [async] accept an optional 
 19â†’[CoroutineContext](https://kotlinlang.org/api/latest/jvm/stdlib/kotlin.coroutines/-coroutine-context/) 
 20â†’parameter that can be used to explicitly specify the dispatcher for the new coroutine and other context elements. 
 21â†’
 22â†’Try the following example:
 23â†’
 24â†’```kotlin
 25â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
 26â†’
 27â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
 28â†’//sampleStart
 29â†’    launch { // context of the parent, main runBlocking coroutine
 30â†’        println("main runBlocking      : I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 31â†’    }
 32â†’    launch(Dispatchers.Unconfined) { // not confined -- will work with main thread
 33â†’        println("Unconfined            : I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 34â†’    }
 35â†’    launch(Dispatchers.Default) { // will get dispatched to DefaultDispatcher 
 36â†’        println("Default               : I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 37â†’    }
 38â†’    launch(newSingleThreadContext("MyOwnThread")) { // will get its own new thread
 39â†’        println("newSingleThreadContext: I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 40â†’    }
 41â†’//sampleEnd    
 42â†’}
 43â†’```
 44â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
 45â†’
 46â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-01.kt).
 47â†’>
 48â†’{type="note"}
 49â†’
 50â†’It produces the following output (maybe in different order):
 51â†’
 52â†’```text
 53â†’Unconfined            : I'm working in thread main
 54â†’Default               : I'm working in thread DefaultDispatcher-worker-1
 55â†’newSingleThreadContext: I'm working in thread MyOwnThread
 56â†’main runBlocking      : I'm working in thread main
 57â†’```
 58â†’
 59â†’<!--- TEST LINES_START_UNORDERED -->
 60â†’
 61â†’When `launch { ... }` is used without parameters, it inherits the context (and thus dispatcher)
 62â†’from the [CoroutineScope] it is being launched from. In this case, it inherits the
 63â†’context of the main `runBlocking` coroutine which runs in the `main` thread. 
 64â†’
 65â†’[Dispatchers.Unconfined] is a special dispatcher that also appears to run in the `main` thread, but it is,
 66â†’in fact, a different mechanism that is explained later.
 67â†’
 68â†’The default dispatcher is used when no other dispatcher is explicitly specified in the scope.
 69â†’It is represented by [Dispatchers.Default] and uses a shared background pool of threads.
 70â†’  
 71â†’[newSingleThreadContext] creates a thread for the coroutine to run. 
 72â†’A dedicated thread is a very expensive resource. 
 73â†’In a real application it must be either released, when no longer needed, using the [close][ExecutorCoroutineDispatcher.close] 
 74â†’function, or stored in a top-level variable and reused throughout the application.  
 75â†’
 76â†’## Unconfined vs confined dispatcher
 77â†’ 
 78â†’The [Dispatchers.Unconfined] coroutine dispatcher starts a coroutine in the caller thread, but only until the
 79â†’first suspension point. After suspension it resumes the coroutine in the thread that is fully determined by the
 80â†’suspending function that was invoked. The unconfined dispatcher is appropriate for coroutines which neither
 81â†’consume CPU time nor update any shared data (like UI) confined to a specific thread. 
 82â†’
 83â†’On the other side, the dispatcher is inherited from the outer [CoroutineScope] by default. 
 84â†’The default dispatcher for the [runBlocking] coroutine, in particular,
 85â†’is confined to the invoker thread, so inheriting it has the effect of confining execution to
 86â†’this thread with predictable FIFO scheduling.
 87â†’
 88â†’```kotlin
 89â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
 90â†’
 91â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
 92â†’//sampleStart
 93â†’    launch(Dispatchers.Unconfined) { // not confined -- will work with main thread
 94â†’        println("Unconfined      : I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 95â†’        delay(500)
 96â†’        println("Unconfined      : After delay in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 97â†’    }
 98â†’    launch { // context of the parent, main runBlocking coroutine
 99â†’        println("main runBlocking: I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
100â†’        delay(1000)
101â†’        println("main runBlocking: After delay in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
102â†’    }
103â†’//sampleEnd    
104â†’}
105â†’```
106â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
107â†’
108â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-02.kt).
109â†’>
110â†’{type="note"}
111â†’
112â†’Produces the output: 
113â†’ 
114â†’```text
115â†’Unconfined      : I'm working in thread main
116â†’main runBlocking: I'm working in thread main
117â†’Unconfined      : After delay in thread kotlinx.coroutines.DefaultExecutor
118â†’main runBlocking: After delay in thread main
119â†’```
120â†’
121â†’<!--- TEST LINES_START -->
122â†’ 
123â†’So, the coroutine with the context inherited from `runBlocking {...}` continues to execute
124â†’in the `main` thread, while the unconfined one resumes in the default executor thread that the [delay]
125â†’function is using.
126â†’
127â†’> The unconfined dispatcher is an advanced mechanism that can be helpful in certain corner cases where
128â†’> dispatching of a coroutine for its execution later is not needed or produces undesirable side-effects,
129â†’> because some operation in a coroutine must be performed right away. 
130â†’> The unconfined dispatcher should not be used in general code. 
131â†’>
132â†’{type="note"}
133â†’
134â†’## Debugging coroutines and threads
135â†’
136â†’Coroutines can suspend on one thread and resume on another thread. 
137â†’Even with a single-threaded dispatcher it might be hard to
138â†’figure out what the coroutine was doing, where, and when if you don't have special tooling. 
139â†’
140â†’### Debugging with IDEA
141â†’
142â†’The Coroutine Debugger of the Kotlin plugin simplifies debugging coroutines in IntelliJ IDEA.
143â†’
144â†’> Debugging works for versions 1.3.8 or later of `kotlinx-coroutines-core`.
145â†’>
146â†’{type="note"}
147â†’
148â†’The **Debug** tool window contains the **Coroutines** tab. In this tab, you can find information about both currently running and suspended coroutines. 
149â†’The coroutines are grouped by the dispatcher they are running on.
150â†’
151â†’![Debugging coroutines](coroutine-idea-debugging-1.png){width=700}
152â†’
153â†’With the coroutine debugger, you can:
154â†’* Check the state of each coroutine.
155â†’* See the values of local and captured variables for both running and suspended coroutines.
156â†’* See a full coroutine creation stack, as well as a call stack inside the coroutine. The stack includes all frames with 
157â†’variable values, even those that would be lost during standard debugging.
158â†’* Get a full report that contains the state of each coroutine and its stack. To obtain it, right-click inside the **Coroutines** tab, and then click **Get Coroutines Dump**.
159â†’
160â†’To start coroutine debugging, you just need to set breakpoints and run the application in debug mode.
161â†’
162â†’Learn more about coroutines debugging in the [tutorial](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/tutorials/coroutines/debug-coroutines-with-idea.html).
163â†’
164â†’### Debugging using logging
165â†’
166â†’Another approach to debugging applications with 
167â†’threads without Coroutine Debugger is to print the thread name in the log file on each log statement. This feature is universally supported
168â†’by logging frameworks. When using coroutines, the thread name alone does not give much of a context, so 
169â†’`kotlinx.coroutines` includes debugging facilities to make it easier. 
170â†’
171â†’Run the following code with `-Dkotlinx.coroutines.debug` JVM option:
172â†’
173â†’```kotlin
174â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
175â†’
176â†’fun log(msg: String) = println("[${Thread.currentThread().name}] $msg")
177â†’
178â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
179â†’//sampleStart
180â†’    val a = async {
181â†’        log("I'm computing a piece of the answer")
182â†’        6
183â†’    }
184â†’    val b = async {
185â†’        log("I'm computing another piece of the answer")
186â†’        7
187â†’    }
188â†’    log("The answer is ${a.await() * b.await()}")
189â†’//sampleEnd    
190â†’}
191â†’```
192â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
193â†’
194â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-03.kt).
195â†’>
196â†’{type="note"}
197â†’
198â†’There are three coroutines. The main coroutine (#1) inside `runBlocking` 
199â†’and two coroutines computing the deferred values `a` (#2) and `b` (#3).
200â†’They are all executing in the context of `runBlocking` and are confined to the main thread.
201â†’The output of this code is:
202â†’
203â†’```text
204â†’[main @coroutine#2] I'm computing a piece of the answer
205â†’[main @coroutine#3] I'm computing another piece of the answer
206â†’[main @coroutine#1] The answer is 42
207â†’```
208â†’
209â†’<!--- TEST FLEXIBLE_THREAD -->
210â†’
211â†’The `log` function prints the name of the thread in square brackets, and you can see that it is the `main`
212â†’thread with the identifier of the currently executing coroutine appended to it. This identifier 
213â†’is consecutively assigned to all created coroutines when the debugging mode is on.
214â†’
215â†’> Debugging mode is also turned on when JVM is run with `-ea` option.
216â†’> You can read more about debugging facilities in the documentation of the [DEBUG_PROPERTY_NAME] property.
217â†’>
218â†’{type="note"}
219â†’
220â†’## Jumping between threads
221â†’
222â†’Run the following code with the `-Dkotlinx.coroutines.debug` JVM option (see [debug](#debugging-coroutines-and-threads)):
223â†’
224â†’```kotlin
225â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
226â†’
227â†’fun log(msg: String) = println("[${Thread.currentThread().name}] $msg")
228â†’
229â†’fun main() {
230â†’//sampleStart
231â†’    newSingleThreadContext("Ctx1").use { ctx1 ->
232â†’        newSingleThreadContext("Ctx2").use { ctx2 ->
233â†’            runBlocking(ctx1) {
234â†’                log("Started in ctx1")
235â†’                withContext(ctx2) {
236â†’                    log("Working in ctx2")
237â†’                }
238â†’                log("Back to ctx1")
239â†’            }
240â†’        }
241â†’    }
242â†’//sampleEnd    
243â†’}
244â†’```
245â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
246â†’
247â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-04.kt).
248â†’>
249â†’{type="note"}
250â†’
251â†’It demonstrates several new techniques. One is using [runBlocking] with an explicitly specified context, and
252â†’the other one is using the [withContext] function to change the context of a coroutine while still staying in the
253â†’same coroutine, as you can see in the output below:
254â†’
255â†’```text
256â†’[Ctx1 @coroutine#1] Started in ctx1
257â†’[Ctx2 @coroutine#1] Working in ctx2
258â†’[Ctx1 @coroutine#1] Back to ctx1
259â†’```
260â†’
261â†’<!--- TEST -->
262â†’
263â†’Note that this example also uses the `use` function from the Kotlin standard library to release threads
264â†’created with [newSingleThreadContext] when they are no longer needed. 
265â†’
266â†’## Job in the context
267â†’
268â†’The coroutine's [Job] is part of its context, and can be retrieved from it 
269â†’using the `coroutineContext[Job]` expression:
270â†’
271â†’```kotlin
272â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
273â†’
274â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
275â†’//sampleStart
276â†’    println("My job is ${coroutineContext[Job]}")
277â†’//sampleEnd    
278â†’}
279â†’```
280â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
281â†’
282â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-05.kt).
283â†’> 
284â†’{type="note"}
285â†’
286â†’In the [debug mode](#debugging-coroutines-and-threads), it outputs something like this:
287â†’
288â†’```
289â†’My job is "coroutine#1":BlockingCoroutine{Active}@6d311334
290â†’```
291â†’

293â†’
294â†’Note that [isActive] in [CoroutineScope] is just a convenient shortcut for
295â†’`coroutineContext[Job]?.isActive == true`.

297â†’## Children of a coroutine
298â†’
299â†’When a coroutine is launched in the [CoroutineScope] of another coroutine,
300â†’it inherits its context via [CoroutineScope.coroutineContext] and 
301â†’the [Job] of the new coroutine becomes
302â†’a _child_ of the parent coroutine's job. When the parent coroutine is cancelled, all its children
303â†’are recursively cancelled, too. 
304â†’
305â†’However, this parent-child relation can be explicitly overriden in one of two ways:
306â†’
307â†’1. When a different scope is explicitly specified when launching a coroutine (for example, `GlobalScope.launch`), 
308â†’   then it does not inherit a `Job` from the parent scope.
309â†’2. When a different `Job` object is passed as the context for the new coroutine (as shown in the example below),
310â†’   then it overrides the `Job` of the parent scope.
311â†’   
312â†’In both cases, the launched coroutine is not tied to the scope it was launched from and operates independently.
313â†’
314â†’```kotlin
315â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
316â†’
317â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
318â†’//sampleStart
319â†’    // launch a coroutine to process some kind of incoming request
320â†’    val request = launch {
321â†’        // it spawns two other jobs
322â†’        launch(Job()) { 
323â†’            println("job1: I run in my own Job and execute independently!")
324â†’            delay(1000)
325â†’            println("job1: I am not affected by cancellation of the request")
326â†’        }
327â†’        // and the other inherits the parent context
328â†’        launch {
329â†’            delay(100)
330â†’            println("job2: I am a child of the request coroutine")
331â†’            delay(1000)
332â†’            println("job2: I will not execute this line if my parent request is cancelled")
333â†’        }
334â†’    }
335â†’    delay(500)
336â†’    request.cancel() // cancel processing of the request
337â†’    println("main: Who has survived request cancellation?")
338â†’    delay(1000) // delay the main thread for a second to see what happens
339â†’//sampleEnd
340â†’}
341â†’```
342â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
343â†’
344â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-06.kt).
345â†’>
346â†’{type="note"}
347â†’
348â†’The output of this code is:
349â†’
350â†’```text
351â†’job1: I run in my own Job and execute independently!
352â†’job2: I am a child of the request coroutine
353â†’main: Who has survived request cancellation?
354â†’job1: I am not affected by cancellation of the request
355â†’```
356â†’
357â†’<!--- TEST -->

359â†’## Parental responsibilities 
360â†’
361â†’A parent coroutine always waits for completion of all its children. A parent does not have to explicitly track
362â†’all the children it launches, and it does not have to use [Job.join] to wait for them at the end:
363â†’
364â†’```kotlin
365â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
366â†’
367â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
368â†’//sampleStart
369â†’    // launch a coroutine to process some kind of incoming request
370â†’    val request = launch {
371â†’        repeat(3) { i -> // launch a few children jobs
372â†’            launch  {
373â†’                delay((i + 1) * 200L) // variable delay 200ms, 400ms, 600ms
374â†’                println("Coroutine $i is done")
375â†’            }
376â†’        }
377â†’        println("request: I'm done and I don't explicitly join my children that are still active")
378â†’    }
379â†’    request.join() // wait for completion of the request, including all its children
380â†’    println("Now processing of the request is complete")
381â†’//sampleEnd
382â†’}
383â†’```
384â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
385â†’
386â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-07.kt).
387â†’>
388â†’{type="note"}
389â†’
390â†’The result is going to be:
391â†’
392â†’```text
393â†’request: I'm done and I don't explicitly join my children that are still active
394â†’Coroutine 0 is done
395â†’Coroutine 1 is done
396â†’Coroutine 2 is done
397â†’Now processing of the request is complete
398â†’```
399â†’
400â†’<!--- TEST -->

402â†’## Naming coroutines for debugging
403â†’
404â†’Automatically assigned ids are good when coroutines log often and you just need to correlate log records
405â†’coming from the same coroutine. However, when a coroutine is tied to the processing of a specific request
406â†’or doing some specific background task, it is better to name it explicitly for debugging purposes.
407â†’The [CoroutineName] context element serves the same purpose as the thread name. It is included in the thread name that
408â†’is executing this coroutine when the [debugging mode](#debugging-coroutines-and-threads) is turned on.
409â†’
410â†’The following example demonstrates this concept:
411â†’
412â†’```kotlin
413â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
414â†’
415â†’fun log(msg: String) = println("[${Thread.currentThread().name}] $msg")
416â†’
417â†’fun main() = runBlocking(CoroutineName("main")) {
418â†’//sampleStart
419â†’    log("Started main coroutine")
420â†’    // run two background value computations
421â†’    val v1 = async(CoroutineName("v1coroutine")) {
422â†’        delay(500)
423â†’        log("Computing v1")
424â†’        252
425â†’    }
426â†’    val v2 = async(CoroutineName("v2coroutine")) {
427â†’        delay(1000)
428â†’        log("Computing v2")
429â†’        6
430â†’    }
431â†’    log("The answer for v1 / v2 = ${v1.await() / v2.await()}")
432â†’//sampleEnd    
433â†’}
434â†’```
435â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
436â†’
437â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-08.kt).
438â†’>
439â†’{type="note"}
440â†’
441â†’The output it produces with `-Dkotlinx.coroutines.debug` JVM option is similar to:
442â†’ 
443â†’```text
444â†’[main @main#1] Started main coroutine
445â†’[main @v1coroutine#2] Computing v1
446â†’[main @v2coroutine#3] Computing v2
447â†’[main @main#1] The answer for v1 / v2 = 42
448â†’```
449â†’
450â†’<!--- TEST FLEXIBLE_THREAD -->

452â†’## Combining context elements
453â†’
454â†’Sometimes we need to define multiple elements for a coroutine context. We can use the `+` operator for that.
455â†’For example, we can launch a coroutine with an explicitly specified dispatcher and an explicitly specified 
456â†’name at the same time: 
457â†’
458â†’```kotlin
459â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
460â†’
461â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
462â†’//sampleStart
463â†’    launch(Dispatchers.Default + CoroutineName("test")) {
464â†’        println("I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
465â†’    }
466â†’//sampleEnd    
467â†’}
468â†’```
469â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
470â†’
471â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-09.kt).
472â†’>
473â†’{type="note"}
474â†’
475â†’The output of this code with the `-Dkotlinx.coroutines.debug` JVM option is: 
476â†’
477â†’```text
478â†’I'm working in thread DefaultDispatcher-worker-1 @test#2
479â†’```
